title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
A narrator recounts the history of Kelvin Harry and his sister, Helen. After Helen disappeared, Dr. Kennedy searched the house and found Helen's packed suitcase and clothes, but no body. Kelvin, believing he had hallucinated strangling Helen, agreed to enter a private sanatorium in Norfolk for treatment. Two years later, Kelvin died at the sanatorium. Following her disappearance, Helen sent two letters: one from Biarritz, France, stating she was going to Spain, and a second six months later from Florence, addressed to "Miss Kennedy," stating she did not want a divorce but would not grant one without proof. Kelvin chose not to divorce her. Gwendolyn, who is searching for news of Helen, reveals she heard her father confess to strangling Helen.

Jiles and Gwendolyn discuss the inconsistencies in the case. Gwendolyn claims she saw a body in the foyer, whereas Kelvin told Kennedy he strangled Helen in the bedroom. Jiles argues that if Kelvin had killed Helen and buried her, he would not have confessed the crime to Dr. Kennedy, a man he was not close to. Jiles notes that the lack of a death record in the parish register proves Helen did not die of natural causes. Jiles suggests the letters might be forgeries designed to mimic Helen's handwriting and reinforce Kelvin's belief that she had run away. Jiles concludes that the pattern of staged departure—including the packed clothes and timed letters—is typical of a calculated murderer, but this contradicts the behavior of a passionate killer who would confess impulsively. Jiles posits that an unknown factor, "X," is involved in the orchestration of these events.
